title: Castrating and tailing lambs
subtitle: How to use rubber rings correctly in the first week of life.
excerpt: Castration and tail docking by rubber ring are legal in the UK only within the first seven days of life. Both procedures reduce management problems later in the year. Done correctly within the legal window, the lamb shows brief distress and recovers within the hour.
sourceNotes: Animal Welfare Act 2006. The Welfare of Farmed Animals (England) Regulations 2007. AHDB Beef and Lamb, BRP manual on lamb management. NADIS (National Animal Disease Information Service), lamb management factsheet.

glossaryTerms.definition: A plier-like device with four prongs that stretches a small rubber ring wide enough to pass over the scrotum or tail. When the prongs are released the ring contracts tightly, cutting off blood supply to the tissue below it. The tissue dies and drops off within 10 to 14 days. Legal for use in lambs only within the first seven days of life.

Removal of the distal portion of the tail in young lambs to reduce faecal soiling and flystrike risk. Legal by rubber ring in the first seven days. The docked tail must leave sufficient cover to protect the vulva in ewes or the equivalent area in rams.

content.text: Both castration and tail docking by rubber ring are controlled under the Animal Welfare Act 2006 and the Welfare of Farmed Animals Regulations. The seven-day window is a hard legal limit: after day seven, these procedures require a vet and anaesthesia. Plan your lambing calendar so you can process all lambs within the window.
Confirm the lamb has received colostrum and is suckling well before you start. Do not ring a lamb that is weak, cold, or not yet standing. Check the
rings are in date and not brittle. Have a second person restrain the lamb if possible, especially for castration.

Stretch the ring onto the elastrator prongs. Hold the lamb with its back to your body, lift the tail, and position the ring around the tail at the correct point. In ewes and females, the docked tail must be long enough to cover the vulva; in males, long enough to cover the end of the prepuce. Release the ring with the prongs pointing away from the lamb's body so the prongs do not pinch the skin. Check the tail hangs below the ring correctly. The
procedure takes less than a minute.

Stretch the ring and check both testicles have descended into the scrotum before placing the ring. The ring must go above both testicles; if only one is in the scrotum, delay until both are present. Place the ring at the top of the scrotum above both testicles and release. Run a finger around the scrotum below the ring to confirm both testicles are below it and the teats are above it. Lambs show distress for approximately 10 to 20 minutes after ringing and then settle.

Return the lamb to the ewe immediately and check she accepts it. Monitor the ring sites daily for the first 48 hours for signs of infection: heat, swelling beyond the expected tissue, or discharge. The tissue below the ring will swell slightly and then shrink as it dries over 10 to 14 days. If the ring area looks abnormally swollen or smells off after 3 days, contact a vet.

Rubber ring procedures carry a tetanus risk. Lambs from ewes vaccinated against clostridial diseases before lambing will have passive maternal immunity. If the ewe was not vaccinated, the lamb may need a tetanus antitoxin injection at the time of ringing; discuss this with your vet at the start of the lambing season, not in the moment.
